%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.3-1 Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time Signals

> **Section Objective:** Learn how to tell whether a signal is continuous-time or discrete-time by checking which time values it is defined on.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Continuous-time signal
- Discrete-time signal
- Time-domain classification
- Standard notation \(x(t)\) and \(x[n]\)

## 1. Continuous-Time Signal

A **continuous-time signal** is specified for a continuum of time values. The notation \(x(t)\) denotes the signal value at time \(t\), where \(t\) can be any real number in the interval being studied — not just selected instants.

A classic example is the output voltage of a telephone microphone or a video camera: the physical quantity exists and is defined at every moment in time.

### EXAM TRIGGER

If a signal is described as existing at **every instant of time**, write it as \(x(t)\).

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not decide a signal is continuous-time simply because its graph looks smooth. A continuous-time signal may have sharp corners or sudden jumps. The only test is whether **time itself is continuous** — that is, whether the signal is defined for every real value of \(t\).

$$x(t),\quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="web_reference_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Wavelet](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0d/Seismic_Wavelet.svg)
*A continuous-time signal \(x(t)\): the curve is drawn over every point on the time axis, reflecting that the signal is defined at every real instant \(t\).*
%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-reference-source"><a href="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0d/Seismic_Wavelet.svg" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Reference image from Wikipedia</a></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Discrete-Time Signal

A **discrete-time signal** is specified only at separated time values, indexed by an integer \(n\). The notation \(x[n]\) denotes the sample value at index \(n\), where \(n\) is an integer — not a continuously varying time variable.

Representative examples include monthly sales revenue, quarterly GNP figures, and daily stock market averages: values are recorded only at separated reporting times, not at every instant.

### EXAM TRIGGER

If data exists only at daily, monthly, quarterly, or sample-numbered instants, use discrete-time notation \(x[n]\).

### COMMON MISTAKE

Discrete-time does **not** mean the amplitudes must be restricted to 0 and 1. The sample values \(x[n]\) can be any real numbers — the word "discrete" refers to the **time index**, not the amplitude range.

$$x[n],\quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="web_reference_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Digital discrete signal](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Digital.signal.discret.svg)
*A discrete-time signal \(x[n]\): isolated vertical stems appear only at integer indices \(n\), with no values defined between them.*
%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-reference-source"><a href="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Digital.signal.discret.svg" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Reference image from Wikimedia Commons</a></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Worked Classification Example

Apply the time-domain test to three familiar signals:

**Telephone microphone voltage** — The electrical voltage produced by a microphone varies at every instant of time. Because the signal is defined over a continuum of \(t\) values, it is a **continuous-time signal**, written \(x(t)\).

**Monthly sales revenue** — A company records total revenue once per month. Values exist only at separated monthly reporting times, so this is a **discrete-time signal**, written \(x[n]\).

**Daily stock market average** — Prices are averaged and recorded once per trading day. Again, values exist only at separated daily instants, making this a **discrete-time signal**, written \(x[n]\).

---

#### Exam Note
- Ask: **What time values are allowed?**
- Do **not** ask first whether the amplitude looks smooth or numerical.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Continuous-time signals use notation \(x(t),\ t\in\mathbb{R}\): the signal is defined for every real time value.
- Discrete-time signals use notation \(x[n],\ n\in\mathbb{Z}\): the signal is defined only at separated integer indices.
- Classification depends on which time values are allowed — not on whether the amplitude looks smooth, jumpy, large, or small.
- Telephone and video camera output are continuous-time; monthly sales and daily stock averages are discrete-time.

*In the next signal classifications, we will separate other properties that are easy to confuse with time discreteness.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
